nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs and reef slopes; perches on exposed rockwork, sponges or coral heads at night with arms spread into the current to filter-feed, and tends to retract into crevices by day.

decorPreferences: Mature live rock with strong vertical relief for the crinoid to perch on and grip with its cirri
Open water column above the rockwork so arms can be extended into the current
Sheltered overhangs and crevices for daytime hiding
Pump and powerhead intakes screened with fine mesh to prevent the arms being shredded

diet.notes: Suspension feeder that extends its feathered arms into the current and collects particles in the 20–150 µm size range — protists, invertebrate larvae, copepods, diatoms and phytoplankton. Most commonly dosed reef phytoplankton products (e.g. Nannochloropsis) are at or below the lower edge of this size range, so a combination of live phytoplankton plus larger particulate foods such as Reef Nutrition Oyster-Feast or Benereef is generally advised. Has been reported to feed best when target-fed with a turkey baster or pipette several times per day, ideally supplemented by an auto-doser/auto-feeder running across the day–night cycle.

goodWith: Most LPS, SPS and soft corals
Non-photosynthetic (NPS) coral systems with heavy plankton dosing
avoidWith: Butterflyfish and other fish known to pick at exposed crinoid arms
Aggressive or boisterous tankmates that disturb its perch

summary: Specialist tropical crinoid sold under the umbrella name 'Feather Star'. Notorious for near-universal captive mortality; only realistic in mature, plankton-dosed specialist systems.
careNotes: The 'Feather Star' or 'Crinoid' label in the aquarium trade covers a number of tropical Indo-Pacific comatulid crinoids — most commonly Lamprometra palmata (now also treated as Dichrometra palmata) and similar genera such as Heterometra and Comatella — that are rarely identified to species at point of sale. They are obligate suspension feeders that need a near-continuous supply of small (≈20–150 µm) particles in the water column, and in standard reef systems they are widely reported to slowly starve over weeks to months, with arm-shedding (autotomy) typically the first visible sign of decline. Tank-size guidance from retail sources is unhelpfully low (e.g. 55 US gal / ~210 L), but specialist crinoid keepers and NPS-coral references consistently describe long-term success only in larger, mature, heavily-fed systems; we have biased the minimum volume up to 400 L on those grounds. The tank should be mature (generally advised at 6+ months) with good vertical rockwork for the cirri to grip, gentle to moderate flow (excessive flow makes the animal relocate or shred), and all pump intakes screened with fine mesh to prevent the delicate arms being drawn into impellers. Feed by target-feeding live phytoplankton mixed with larger particulate foods such as Oyster-Feast or Benereef several times per day, ideally supplemented by an auto-doser running across the day–night cycle. Like other echinoderms, crinoids are sensitive to copper-based medications and to swings in salinity, pH, temperature and dissolved oxygen, and they tolerate only very low nitrate/phosphate. Source spread on sizing was wide (FreshMarine cited up to ~35 cm for related Himerometra spp., retail listings of Lamprometra sp. quote ~15 cm); we have used a 15–25 cm armspan range consistent with Lamprometra palmata specifically. Given the near-universal captive failure rate, this entry is flagged as specialist-only.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Assigned primary species page (article.cfm?aid=194 'Marine Aquarium Inverts: Echinoderms — Feather Stars') returned HTTP 404 at access time. Used the LiveAquaria root as the citation anchor and pulled equivalent retail-care data from a related retail listing (FreshMarine Himerometra) plus specialist hobby references; cross-source agreement on care difficulty, planktivore diet and feeding regime was strong.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Assigned secondary source returned HTTP 403 (Cloudflare bot wall). Swapped to alternative secondaries below.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Lamprometra palmata / Dichrometra palmata
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Mariametridae, order Comatulida, class Crinoidea) and for the Dichrometra palmata synonymy.

additional.name: WoRMS — Lamprometra palmata (Müller, 1841)
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy: WoRMS lists Lamprometra palmata as an unaccepted synonym of Dichrometra palmata; family Mariametridae, order Comatulida. Distribution Indo-Pacific (W India, Maldives, Bay of Bengal, East Indies, N Australia, Philippines, S Japan, S Pacific). Depth 0–51 m (questionable 433 m record).

additional.name: Live Rock n Reef — Red Feather Starfish (Lamprometra sp.)
additional.notes: Lamprometra-specific retail care: temp 74–82 °F, pH 7.9–8.5, SG 1.020–1.027, listed min tank 55 US gal (~208 L), adult size ~15 cm, expert care. Cross-checked against FreshMarine's Himerometra Feather Sea Star guide (72–78 °F, pH 8.10–8.40, SG 1.023–1.025) — averaged into the temp/pH/SG ranges in this file. Tank-size figure treated as a retail-floor outlier; per playbook biased upward toward 400 L for long-term success in a plankton-rich system.

additional.name: Reef Builders — Are Feather Stars STILL Best Left in the Ocean? (2024)
additional.notes: Independent specialist reference confirming near-universal captive failure, ~20–150 µm particle-size requirement, gentle-to-moderate flow, incompatibility with standard SPS-dominated tanks, and arm-shedding as the first sign of starvation. Anchors the specialist-only advisory.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Near-universal captive mortality; specialist phytoplankton dosing and large mature systems required for any chance of survival.
